Amund Hero Review: Is The Bone Carver Worth It?
Amund brings a massive 350% hit-all and a unique cleanse-blocking fiend to the table. But with stats from early 2025, can he still compete with the modern gods of E&P? We analyze his viability.
Amund Review: Bone-Breaking Fiends or Just a Pile of Calcium?
Amund: The Tenacious Tracker Who Wants to Pick a Bone With You
Amund, the "Tenacious Tracker," arrived in February 2025 as part of the Plains Hunter family (Monster Island event). He’s a

hero designed to be a disruptor and a damage dealer. His main gimmick involves dealing solid damage to everyone and then slapping them with a "Bone Trap Fiend" that not only eats healing but also prevents the enemy from cleansing ailments. Think of him as the guy who locks the bathroom door from the outside after you've eaten bad sushi.

Amund looks like he raided a dinosaur museum and decided to wear the exhibits. He is decked out in what appears to be chitinous, bony armor with massive horns and spikes protruding from every angle. He carries a gigantic bundle of dark, ribbed tusk-like objects on his shoulder—perhaps trophies or just his camping gear for Monster Island. The color palette is earthy and golden, fitting his Holy element, but with a rugged, primal twist that separates him from the usual shiny, angelic yellow heroes. It's a very "Monster Hunter" aesthetic: practical, intimidating, and slightly excessive.
At his release in early 2025, Amund's stats were respectable. However, looking at him now in 2026, he is showing his age.
. Modern hitters often push past 1300 raw attack.
. Current tanks and supports are sitting comfortably in the 1300+ range.
. While over 2000 HP is decent, new heroes are pushing 2300-2400 base.

He falls into the "Glass Cannon" territory by modern standards, though "Glass Musket" might be more accurate given the power creep. He needs emblems and Limit Breaks to survive the opening volley of a modern war.
"Carve the Bones" is a deceptively simple but tactically deep skill.
Even with his slightly dated attack stat, a 350% multiplier to all enemies is significant. It softens up the entire enemy team, bringing them into kill range for your snipers.
This is the star of the show.
It starts at 45% attack and ramps up. It's a ticking time bomb.
is a massive amount. For a modern hero with 3000 HP (after troops), that fiend needs to absorb nearly 1350 healing before it vanishes. That effectively nullifies one or two healer casts.
This is the utility gold. As long as the fiend is active, the enemy

. If you pair him with a heavy ailment giver (like a silencer or defense down hero), the enemy is stuck with those ailments until they heal off the massive fiend. It creates a "Catch-22": they need to heal to remove the fiend to cleanse, but the fiend eats the heal.
With a lvl 23 Mana Troop, he drops to 9 tiles.
With the 2025 Plains Hunter family bonus (up to +20% Attack, but no mana bonus), he relies entirely on troops and passives for speed.
With ~1350 Attack (LB1 + Emblems) vs ~1200 Defense enemies:

Family Bonus, Passives, Aether Power & Unique Abilities
The Plains Hunter family bonus is purely offensive:

. It's a simple stat stick. While nice, it lacks the utility of mana generation or healing bonuses found in other families. It encourages stacking multiple hunters, which can be devastating for tile damage but risky for defense.
A 10% chance to drop received special damage to 1. It’s unreliable (RNG dependent), but when it procs against a sniper like Turundh, it feels like winning the lottery.
Damage over Time on Special (Hunter's Mark):
Adds a small DoT (150 damage over 3 turns). It’s negligible damage but counts as an ailment, which can interact with other hero skills (like those dealing extra damage per ailment).

This allows Amund to resist incoming fiends at the start of the battle. It's a niche power. Useful against specific defenses (like those running Ulius or older fiend metas), but generally less universally useful than "Defense Up" or "Regen" for a hero with his stat line.
Upon release, Amund was a "must-have" counter to heavy sustain metas. His high-HP fiends shut down healers like Hathor and Rhys effectively.

The game has moved fast. Modern heroes released in late 2025 (like the Construct or Magic Tower waves) have ~10-15% higher base stats. His 350% hit, while decent, is no longer a guaranteed team-wipe threat on its own.
where his Average speed becomes Very Fast. The fiend is still a nightmare to remove without a specific "Minion/Fiend Destroyer" hero.
He has transitioned from a main damage dealer to a "Utility Attacker." You bring him not just to kill, but to ensure your other ailments (Defense Down, Silence) stick permanently.
, giving him the chance to Revive (which pairs hilariously well with his damage reduction passive—he refuses to die).
His base survivability is low by modern standards. He needs to live long enough to fire. Maximizing defense and health ensures he can survive a hit from faster enemies.
His hit will be slightly softer.
Maximizes the 350% hit and the fiend damage (which scales off attack).
He becomes very fragile and might die before firing in standard speed matches.
path is recommended. His value is in the Fiend application; if he dies with full mana, he does nothing.
Unique ability to lock ailments on enemies, forcing them to heal (which is blocked) before they can cleanse.
The fiend eats 45% of max HP worth of healing. This is one of the highest healing absorbs in the game.
350% to all is still a strong benchmark for Average speed.
The revive talent can clutch close games.
His survivability is suspect against the newest 1300+ Attack monsters.
who destroy fiends or heal heavily can neutralize his primary gimmick instantly.
Without mana troops or rush modes, he can be too slow against Very Fast rush teams.
He is a beast here. Firing early means the enemy is locked out of cleansing and healing from the start.
Specifically against heavy healer/cleanser teams. Pair him with a Defense Down hero to lock the debuff in.
The fiend damage racks up, and while the healing absorb is irrelevant (no healing allowed), the cleanse block still works!
Amund fires, applying fiends. Kullervo hits, applying Mindless Attack. The enemy usually waits for a cleanser, but Amund's fiend prevents the cleanse. They are forced to attack themselves.
Rhys speeds everyone up. Amund softens and blocks healing. Ogima cleans up minions and deals heavy damage.
(Ailment immunity prevents the cleanse block if cast first), and the new
Do not pair with heroes who rely on the enemy

buffs to steal (like El Naddaha), as Amund doesn't dispel, but he also doesn't clash. Avoid pairing with weaker fiend summoners, as he might overwrite a useful effect, though his fiends are generally superior.
If you pulled Amund early, congratulations. He is a PvE monster. The fiends will shut down bosses who heal, and his hit-all damage will clear map stages easily. Max him ASAP.

Amund is a situational tool. He won't be on your primary defense team anymore due to his stats. However, keep him for War depth, especially for Rush wars or against stall teams relying on Vivica/Ariel/Cleansers. Do not LB2 him unless you use him daily; LB1 is sufficient for his role.
- One player noted his dominance in tournaments where healing is disabled, but the fiend damage and cleanse block remain.
- Comparing him to another heavy hitter, highlighting his offensive nature.
"This guy and Whitefang would be very annoying combo."
- Players immediately recognized the synergy with other ailment-heavy heroes.
Many players pointed out that Arco (a fiend destroyer) is a hard counter, making Amund risky on defense if the attacker has him.

Amund is like a heavy metal album cover come to life—loud, aggressive, and full of bones. While his stats aren't top-chart material in 2026, his specific skill set (Massive Hit + Cleanse Block Fiend) ensures he stays in the rotation for savvy players. He effectively turns off the enemy's ability to "reset" the fight.
A solid B+ hero in the modern era, rising to A-tier in Rush modes. Keep those bones sharp!
At the time of this review, Amund does not have a costume. If one is released, look for stat bumps to bring him back to A+ territory.
Detailed review of Amund, the 5-star Holy hero from the Monster Hunter family in Empires & Puzzles. Analysis of stats, skills, emblem paths, and modern viability.
